source_topic: How path tracing renders photorealistic light, one ray at a time

scenes.narration: Your graphics engine doesn't start from a light source. It starts from your eye — and works backwards through space.
One ray fires from your camera, bounces off each surface it hits, then hunts for a light source. That entire zigzag path is one sample of reality.

props.fallbackText: One ray bounces from camera to light — that's one sample of reality.

scenes.narration: But one sample is noise. So your GPU fires a thousand rays per pixel — each one a different random path through the same scene.
Old games fake shadows and reflections with tricks. Path tracing computes them from actual light paths — no faking, pure physics.
The more paths you sample, the more noise cancels out. Halve the noise? You need four times as many rays. That's the math.
For decades this was too slow for real-time games. Then AI denoising arrived — cleaning up noisy frames instantly.

props.text: AI denoising made path tracing real-time.

scenes.narration: That's the physics of light, simulated inside your GPU. Follow for one concept a day.

caption:
Most people think light starts at the source. Path tracing runs it backwards — your GPU fires rays FROM the camera, hunting light sources through thousands of random bounces.

The result? Real shadows. Real reflections. Real physics. No tricks.

And in 2026, AI denoising finally made it fast enough to run in your games.

Save this to explain it next time someone asks why RTX exists.
